questions.question:
Forestland is a planet inhabited by different kinds of creatures. Among other creatures, it is populated by animals all of whom are ferocious. There are also creatures that have claws and some that do not. All creatures that have claws are ferocious.

Based only on the information provided above, which one of the following options can be logically inferred with certainty?

A. All creatures with claws are animals.
B. Some creatures with claws are not ferocious.
C. Some non-ferocious creatures are animals.
D. Some ferocious creatures are creatures with claws.
questions.answer: D. Some ferocious creatures are creatures with claws.

understand.plain: The passage gives us two facts: (1) all animals are ferocious, and (2) all creatures with claws are ferocious. We need to find which conclusion follows with certainty from these two statements alone. The question tests syllogistic reasoning — drawing only what the given premises guarantee, without adding assumptions.

keywords.explain: A conclusion that must be true whenever all given premises are true; it cannot merely be possible or probable.
keywords.example: From 'All cats are mammals' and 'Some pets are cats', we can infer 'Some pets are mammals' with certainty.

keywords.explain: Statement P is sufficient for Q when P -> Q; knowing P is enough to guarantee Q.
keywords.example: Having claws is sufficient for being ferocious (all claw-creatures are ferocious), but being ferocious is not sufficient for having claws.

keywords.explain: Incorrectly reversing a conditional: from 'All A are B', falsely concluding 'All B are A'.
keywords.example: From 'All animals are ferocious' it does NOT follow that 'All ferocious creatures are animals'.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><ellipse cx='210' cy='130' rx='185' ry='110' style='fill:none;stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:2;stroke-dasharray:6,3;'/><text x='210' y='20' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>Ferocious Creatures (large set)</text><ellipse cx='150' cy='145' rx='80' ry='60' style='fill:rgba(206,203,246,0.35);stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='150' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Animals</text><text x='150' y='157' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(all ferocious)</text><ellipse cx='275' cy='145' rx='75' ry='55' style='fill:rgba(29,158,117,0.18);stroke:#1D9E75;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='275' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Claw</text><text x='275' y='157' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>creatures</text><text x='275' y='172' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(all ferocious)</text><text x='210' y='235' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Both circles lie inside the Ferocious set — overlap possible but not required</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram showing Animals and Claw-creatures as two overlapping circles, both contained within the larger Ferocious Creatures region.

given.aim: Determine which of the four options is logically certain given the two premises about Forestland creatures.

terms.meaning: The set of animals is a subset of ferocious creatures: Animals -> Ferocious.
terms.example: If Rex is an animal, Rex is ferocious.
terms.connects: Establishes that ferocious set contains all animals.

terms.meaning: The set of claw-creatures is a subset of ferocious creatures: Claws -> Ferocious.
terms.example: If Zara has claws, Zara is ferocious.
terms.connects: Establishes that ferocious set also contains all claw-creatures.

terms.meaning: For the inference to work, the set in question must be non-empty; the premise 'There are also creatures that have claws' guarantees claws is non-empty.
terms.example: Because claw-creatures exist and all of them are ferocious, there exists at least one ferocious creature that has claws.

given.plan: Check each option: (A) requires claws -> animals, not given. (B) contradicts the premise. (C) requires non-ferocious animals, but all animals are ferocious. (D) follows directly from 'all claw-creatures are ferocious' combined with 'claw-creatures exist' => some ferocious creatures have claws.

steps.title: Evaluate option A: All claw-creatures are animals
steps.apply: Premise only says Claws -> Ferocious. There is no link from Claws -> Animals. Option A is not supported.
steps.note: This would be the converse of Animals -> Ferocious conflated with Claws -> Ferocious — a logical error.

steps.title: Evaluate option B: Some claw-creatures are not ferocious
steps.formula_raw: (A -> B) is logically equivalent to (not B -> not A)
steps.apply: Premise states all claw-creatures ARE ferocious, so option B directly contradicts a given premise. Option B is false.
steps.note: Option B is impossible given the premises.

steps.title: Evaluate option C: Some non-ferocious creatures are animals
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (not B), conclude (not A)
steps.apply: All animals are ferocious means Animals -> Ferocious. A non-ferocious animal would contradict this premise. Option C is false.
steps.note: By contrapositive: not-Ferocious -> not-Animal, so non-ferocious creatures cannot be animals.

steps.title: Evaluate option D: Some ferocious creatures have claws
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: The passage states claw-creatures exist. All of them are ferocious. Therefore there exist creatures that are both ferocious AND have claws => 'Some ferocious creatures have claws' is TRUE with certainty.
steps.note: This is a direct existential conclusion: claw-creatures are non-empty and are a subset of ferocious creatures.

solution.result: Option D is the only logically certain inference. Some ferocious creatures are creatures with claws (because claw-creatures exist and all of them are ferocious).

questions.formula_note: The key move is converting the universal 'All claws are ferocious' plus existence of claw-creatures into the existential 'Some ferocious are claw-creatures'.

questions.question:
Given below are two statements and four conclusions drawn based on the statements.

Statement 1: Some bottles are cups.
Statement 2: All cups are knives.

Conclusion I: Some bottles are knives.
Conclusion II: Some knives are cups.
Conclusion III: All cups are bottles.
Conclusion IV: All knives are cups.

Which one of the following options can be logically inferred?

A. Only conclusion I and conclusion II are correct.
B. Only conclusion II and conclusion III are correct.
C. Only conclusion II and conclusion IV are correct.
D. Only conclusion III and conclusion IV are correct.
questions.answer: A. Only conclusion I and conclusion II are correct.

understand.plain: This is a classic two-premise syllogism with 'Some' and 'All' quantifiers. Statement 1 is a particular affirmative (some bottles are cups) and Statement 2 is a universal affirmative (all cups are knives). We need to chain these to derive valid conclusions and reject invalid ones.

keywords.explain: A statement of the form 'Some A are B', meaning at least one element belongs to both A and B.
keywords.example: Statement 1: 'Some bottles are cups' — there is a non-empty intersection of bottles and cups.

keywords.explain: A statement of the form 'All A are B', meaning the set A is entirely contained in set B.
keywords.example: Statement 2: 'All cups are knives' — cups is a subset of knives.

keywords.explain: Swapping subject and predicate: converse of 'All A are B' is 'All B are A', which is NOT generally valid.
keywords.example: From 'All cups are knives' we CANNOT conclude 'All knives are cups' or 'All knives are bottles'.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><ellipse cx='240' cy='130' rx='155' ry='95' style='fill:rgba(206,203,246,0.3);stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='340' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Knives</text><ellipse cx='215' cy='135' rx='90' ry='65' style='fill:rgba(29,158,117,0.2);stroke:#1D9E75;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='265' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Cups</text><text x='265' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(subset of Knives)</text><ellipse cx='145' cy='130' rx='80' ry='55' style='fill:rgba(216,90,48,0.15);stroke:#D85A30;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='100' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Bottles</text><text x='178' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut' style='font-size:11px;'>overlap</text><text x='210' y='240' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Bottles intersects Cups (stmt 1); Cups inside Knives (stmt 2) => overlap of Bottles and Knives exists</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram with Knives as the outer ellipse, Cups as a smaller ellipse inside Knives, and Bottles as an ellipse that overlaps with Cups, showing that the overlapping region of Bottles and Cups lies inside Knives.

given.aim: Determine which of the four conclusions follow necessarily from the two given statements using standard syllogistic rules.

terms.meaning: There is a non-empty intersection between Bottles and Cups sets.
terms.example: Let object X be both a bottle and a cup.
terms.connects: The overlap region Bottles∩Cups is non-empty.

terms.meaning: Cups is a strict subset of Knives; every cup is also a knife.
terms.example: Object X (a cup) is also a knife.
terms.connects: Since X is in Bottles∩Cups and Cups ⊆ Knives, X is also in Knives => some bottles are knives.

terms.meaning: Reversing 'All A are B' to 'All B are A' is invalid; it does not follow from the given premises.
terms.example: From 'All cups are knives', we cannot say 'All knives are cups' (Conclusion IV is wrong) or 'All cups are bottles' (Conclusion III is wrong).

given.plan: Chain Statement 1 and Statement 2: the overlap of Bottles and Cups (non-empty by Stmt 1) is inside Knives (by Stmt 2), so Some bottles are knives (Conclusion I). Cups are a subset of Knives and non-empty, so Some knives are cups (Conclusion II, by conversion of A-type which is valid for particular). Conclusion III reverses Stmt 1 (invalid). Conclusion IV reverses Stmt 2 (invalid).

steps.title: Chain statements to test Conclusion I
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (B -> C), conclude (A -> C)
steps.apply: Some bottles are cups (Stmt 1) + All cups are knives (Stmt 2). Let X be an object in Bottles∩Cups. Since X is a cup and all cups are knives, X is also a knife. So X is in Bottles∩Knives => Some bottles are knives. Conclusion I is CORRECT.
steps.note: This is a valid Barbara syllogism variant: the particular premise carries through the universal.

steps.title: Test Conclusion II: Some knives are cups
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = (exists x)(A(x) and B(x))
steps.apply: From Stmt 2: All cups are knives. Cups set is non-empty (Stmt 1 says some bottles are cups, so cups exist). Therefore there exist objects that are simultaneously cups and knives => Some knives are cups. Conclusion II is CORRECT.
steps.note: The valid converse of a universal-affirmative does not hold in general, but here we use the particular form: cups exist (non-empty) and all cups are knives, so 'some knives are cups' follows.

steps.title: Test Conclusion III: All cups are bottles
steps.apply: Stmt 1 says 'Some bottles are cups', not 'All cups are bottles'. The converse is not licensed. Conclusion III is INCORRECT.
steps.note: A particular statement 'Some A are B' does not imply 'All B are A'.

steps.title: Test Conclusion IV: All knives are cups
steps.formula_raw: from (A -> B) and (not A), you may NOT conclude (not B)
steps.apply: Stmt 2 says 'All cups are knives' (Cups ⊆ Knives). Reversing this to 'All knives are cups' (Knives ⊆ Cups) is the converse fallacy. Conclusion IV is INCORRECT.
steps.note: The universal affirmative conversion (A -> B becomes B -> A) is invalid in classical logic.

solution.result: Conclusions I and II are correct; III and IV are not. The answer is A.

questions.formula_note: The valid syllogistic chain uses the non-empty intersection from the particular premise to propagate through the universal, while converse inferences on universal-affirmative statements are blocked.

questions.question:
The letters P, Q, R, S, T and U are to be placed one per vertex on a regular convex hexagon, but not necessarily in the same order.

Consider the following statements:
- The line segment joining R and S is longer than the line segment joining P and Q.
- The line segment joining R and U is perpendicular to the line segment joining P and T.
- The line segment joining R and U is parallel to the line segment joining Q and T.

Which of the given statements is/are CORRECT?

A. Statement 1 and Statement 2
B. Statement 2 only
C. Statement 1 only
D. Statement 3 only

understand.plain: Six letters are placed at the six vertices of a regular convex hexagon. We must figure out a consistent placement from the three statements about relative distances and perpendicularity/parallelism of line segments connecting specific pairs of vertices. A regular hexagon has three distinct inter-vertex distances: adjacent (side length s), two-apart (s*sqrt(3)), and opposite (diameter, 2s).

keywords.explain: A six-sided polygon with all sides equal and all interior angles equal (120 degrees). Vertices are evenly spaced on a circle.
keywords.example: Label vertices 1-6 around the circle; adjacent vertices are distance s apart, skip-one vertices s*sqrt(3) apart, opposite vertices 2s apart.

keywords.explain: Two segments that meet at a 90-degree angle when extended; in the hexagon context, certain diagonals are perpendicular to others based on the 60-degree vertex spacing.
keywords.example: In a regular hexagon centred at origin with vertices at 0, 60, 120, 180, 240, 300 degrees, the segment from vertex at 0 deg to vertex at 180 deg is perpendicular to the segment from vertex at 90 deg to vertex at 270 deg (impossible in hexagon), so perpendicularity depends on the chosen vertex positions.

keywords.explain: Two segments that have the same direction (slope). In a regular hexagon, segments connecting vertices that are symmetric about the centre are parallel.
keywords.example: The segment from vertex 1 to vertex 3 is parallel to the segment from vertex 4 to vertex 6 (both skip one vertex on the same side).

UI,sans-serif;}</style><polygon points='210,50 270,160 210,270 90,270 30,160 90,50' style='fill:none;stroke:#B4B2A9;stroke-width:2;'/><circle cx='210' cy='50' r='14' style='fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='210' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>P</text><circle cx='270' cy='160' r='14' style='fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='270' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Q</text><circle cx='210' cy='270' r='14' style='fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:#B4B2A9;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='210' y='275' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R</text><circle cx='90' cy='270' r='14' style='fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:#B4B2A9;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='90' y='275' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>S</text><circle cx='30' cy='160' r='14' style='fill:rgba(29,158,117,0.25);stroke:#1D9E75;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='30' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>T</text><circle cx='90' cy='50' r='14' style='fill:rgba(29,158,117,0.25);stroke:#1D9E75;stroke-width:2;'/><text x='90' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>U</text><line x1='210' y1='270' x2='90' y2='270' style='stroke:#D85A30;stroke-width:2.5;stroke-dasharray:5,3;'/><line x1='210' y1='50' x2='270' y2='160' style='stroke:#7F77DD;stroke-width:2.5;stroke-dasharray:5,3;'/><line x1='210' y1='270' x2='90' y2='50' style='stroke:#1D9E75;stroke-width:2;'/><line x1='210' y1='50' x2='30' y2='160' style='stroke:#1D9E75;stroke-width:2;stroke-dasharray:4,3;'/><text x='160' y='310' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>One valid placement; R-S adjacent (short), P-Q adjacent (same) — see solution</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Regular hexagon with vertices labelled P at top-right, Q at right, R at bottom-right, S at bottom-left, T at left, U at top-left, showing the relevant line segments.

given.aim: Find which of the three statements about line segments on a regular hexagon can simultaneously be true for some valid placement of P, Q, R, S, T, U.

terms.meaning: In a regular hexagon with side s, adjacent vertices are distance s, vertices two apart are s*sqrt(3), and opposite vertices are 2s.
terms.example: Vertices numbered 1 to 6: dist(1,2)=s (adjacent), dist(1,3)=s*sqrt(3) (skip one), dist(1,4)=2s (opposite).
terms.connects: Determines which placement makes R-S longer than P-Q.

terms.meaning: For two chords to be perpendicular, the angle between their directions must be 90 degrees. With vertices at 60-degree intervals, specific vertex-pair combinations yield perpendicular segments.
terms.example: If vertex A is at angle 0 deg and B at angle 180 deg, AB is horizontal. For CD to be perpendicular, C and D must be at 90 deg and 270 deg — but those positions are not hexagon vertices, so perpendicularity requires skip-one chords at the right angles.
terms.connects: Guides testing Statement 2 for the placement found.

terms.meaning: Two chords are parallel when they connect vertex pairs that differ by the same angular step.
terms.example: Chord from vertex 1 to vertex 3 (angular span +120 deg) is parallel to chord from vertex 4 to vertex 6 (also +120 deg). Chord from vertex 1 to vertex 2 is parallel to chord from vertex 4 to vertex 5.
terms.connects: Tests Statement 3 for the found placement.

given.plan: Assign P, Q, R, S, T, U to vertices 1-6 of the hexagon (at angles 0, 60, 120, 180, 240, 300 degrees from centre). Try a placement consistent with the statements. Check each statement geometrically using coordinates.

steps.title: Set up hexagon coordinates
steps.apply: Place hexagon with centre at origin, radius 1. Vertices at angles 0, 60, 120, 180, 240, 300 degrees: V1=(1,0), V2=(0.5, sqrt(3)/2), V3=(-0.5, sqrt(3)/2), V4=(-1,0), V5=(-0.5,-sqrt(3)/2), V6=(0.5,-sqrt(3)/2). There are 6! = 720 placements; we analyse geometrically.
steps.note: Side length s = 1. Distances: adjacent=1, skip-one=sqrt(3), opposite=2.

steps.title: Try placement: P=V1, Q=V2, R=V3, S=V4, T=V5, U=V6
steps.formula_raw: A[i][j] = 1 if there is an edge from vertex i to vertex j, else 0
steps.apply: P=(1,0), Q=(0.5,sqrt(3)/2), R=(-0.5,sqrt(3)/2), S=(-1,0), T=(-0.5,-sqrt(3)/2), U=(0.5,-sqrt(3)/2). Stmt 1: |RS| = dist(R,S) = dist(V3,V4)=1 (adjacent); |PQ|=dist(V1,V2)=1 (adjacent) => |RS|=|PQ|, NOT longer. Try other placement.
steps.note: Need R and S to be further apart than P and Q.

steps.title: Try placement: P=V1, Q=V2, R=V2... rethink — use opposite/skip-one
steps.formula_raw: in an unweighted (di)graph, the fewest edges from s to t equals the BFS distance
steps.apply: Try P=V1, Q=V2 (adjacent, dist=1), R=V3, S=V6 (skip-two: dist=sqrt(3)~1.73 > 1). Now dist(RS)=dist(V3,V6): V3=(-0.5,sqrt(3)/2), V6=(0.5,-sqrt(3)/2). dist=sqrt(1+3)=2 (opposite!). So dist(RS)=2 > dist(PQ)=1. Statement 1 is TRUE for this placement. Now check Statements 2 and 3 with T=V4, U=V5 (remaining vertices).

steps.title: Check Statement 2: RU perpendicular to PT
steps.formula_raw: A => B is false only when A is true and B is false; otherwise it is true
steps.apply: R=V3=(-0.5, sqrt(3)/2), U=V5=(-0.5,-sqrt(3)/2). Direction vector RU = (0,-sqrt(3)) — vertical. P=V1=(1,0), T=V4=(-1,0). Direction vector PT = (-2,0) — horizontal. Vertical perpendicular to horizontal => RU perp PT. Statement 2 is TRUE.
steps.note: V3 and V5 are both at x=-0.5 so their connecting segment is vertical; V1 and V4 are both at y=0 so their segment is horizontal.

steps.title: Check Statement 3: RU parallel to QT
steps.formula_raw: (A -> B) is logically equivalent to (not B -> not A)
steps.apply: RU direction = (0,-sqrt(3)) (vertical). Q=V2=(0.5,sqrt(3)/2), T=V4=(-1,0). Direction QT = (-1-0.5, 0-sqrt(3)/2) = (-1.5, -sqrt(3)/2). This is NOT vertical (has non-zero x-component). So RU is NOT parallel to QT. Statement 3 is FALSE.
steps.note: For Statement 3 to be true we would need a different placement, but the question asks which combination is correct, not which single statement.

steps.title: Determine the answer
steps.formula_raw: An argument is valid iff the conditional (P_1 /\ ... /\ P_n) -> C is true under every truth assignment
steps.apply: For the placement P=V1,Q=V2,R=V3,S=V6,T=V4,U=V5: Statement 1 TRUE (|RS|=2 > |PQ|=1), Statement 2 TRUE (RU vertical, PT horizontal => perpendicular), Statement 3 FALSE (RU vertical, QT non-vertical => not parallel). So Statement 1 AND Statement 2 are correct. Answer is A.
steps.note: The question asks which statements are correct, and option A says 'Statement 1 and Statement 2', which matches our analysis.

solution.result: Statements 1 and 2 are correct for a valid placement of the six letters on the hexagon vertices. The answer is A.

questions.formula_note: The geometric argument relies on setting up Cartesian coordinates for the hexagon vertices and computing distance and direction vectors to verify each statement.
